mini_headings_covered: Key Concept: A frame of reference is a set of coordinates that can be used to determine positions of objects.
Table 3.1 Difference between distance and displacement
Key Concept: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement.
Key Concept: Average velocity is the total displacement of a body over a time interval.
Key Concept: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.
Key Concept: Motion at a constant velocity or uniform motion means that the position of the object is changing at the same rate.

3.3 Average Velocity and Instantaneous V: Average speed vs average velocity magnitude

subject: PHYSICS
unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: Three students start at the same point. Student A moves 200 m right then 100 m left. Student B moves 200 m right then 300 m left. Student C moves 200 m right then 200 m left. Which student has zero displacement?
questions.explanation: Student C returns exactly to start: 200 m right + 200 m left = 0 displacement. Option A has displacement 100 m right; Option B has displacement 100 m left.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Three students walked on a straight line... first: 200 m right + 100 m left; second: 200 m right + 300 m left; third: 200 m right + 200 m left

explanations_tiered.definition: Displacement is the net change in position from start to finish.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Zero displacement means the object ends at its starting point.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Student C went 200 m right, then 200 m left — back to start, so displacement = 0.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Net displacement = sum of movements with signs. C: +200 − 200 = 0.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Displacement vector sum: \(\vec{S}_{net} = \sum \vec{S}_i\). For C, the vectors cancel exactly.
misconceptions: Thinking distance = displacement for round trips.
Adding magnitudes only (200+200=400) without signs.

flashcard.front: 200 m right then 200 m left: displacement?

visual_system.visual_description: Number line showing three paths: A ends at +100, B at −100, C at 0.
visual_system.caption: Only Student C has zero displacement.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Number line from −100 to +100. A ends at +100, B at −100, C at 0.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 200 m right then 200 m left: displacement?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use activity to show net vs total path.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate net displacement from multiple segments

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: What is the displacement when the final position is the same as the initial position?
questions.explanation: If final position equals initial position, then displacement = 0. Option B is incorrect because distance could be large while displacement is zero.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: What is the displacement if the final position is the same as the initial position?

explanations_tiered.definition: Displacement is final position minus initial position.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When starting and ending points are identical, the net change in position is zero.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk around a track and end where you started, your displacement is zero.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\Delta S = S_f - S_i\). If \(S_f = S_i\), then \(\Delta S = 0\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Displacement is path-independent; only endpoints matter. Identical endpoints give zero vector.
misconceptions: Displacement equals distance when start and end same. Incorrect; distance can be large.
Displacement is not defined when start = end. Incorrect; it is defined as zero.

glossary_entry.definition: Occurs when initial and final positions are equal.

flashcard.front: What is displacement if start = end?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Displacement = final − initial = 0 when they are equal.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. S_f − S_i = 0 when S_f = S_i.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: Given \(S_f = (5,0)\) and \(S_i = (-1,0)\), what is the sign of the net displacement along the x-axis?
questions.explanation: \(\Delta S = S_f - S_i = 5 - (-1) = +6\). The positive sign indicates displacement in the positive x-direction. Option B would be true if S_f < S_i.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Given S_f = (5,0) and S_i = (-1,0), check whether net displacement is positive or negative.

explanations_tiered.definition: Sign of displacement indicates direction relative to chosen positive axis.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Displacement = final − initial. Positive result means displacement in positive direction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 5 minus negative 1 is like 5 + 1 = 6, which is positive, so displacement is positive.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\Delta S = 5 - (-1) = +6\). Positive sign means displacement is in the positive x-direction.
misconceptions: Thinking negative minus positive gives negative (confusing order).
Ignoring that subtracting a negative adds.

glossary_entry.definition: Positive if final > initial on chosen axis.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: S_f = 5, S_i = −1: displacement positive or negative?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that subtracting a negative yields addition.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: What quantity does a car's speedometer directly measure?
questions.hint: Speedometer shows the speed at the current moment.
questions.explanation: A speedometer measures instantaneous speed — the speed at a specific instant. Average speed is total distance over total time, not displayed in real time.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: In Figure ??, what does the speedometer read? (Speedometer measures instantaneous speed)

explanations_tiered.definition: Instantaneous speed is the speed at a particular instant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Speedometer needle moves continuously, showing the real-time speed.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The speedometer shows how fast you are going right now, not the average over the whole trip.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Speedometer readings change instantly with speed; they give instantaneous speed, not average.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Instantaneous speed is the magnitude of instantaneous velocity: \(v = |\vec{v}| = |d\vec{S}/dt|\).
reasoning_steps: It does not compute total distance/time.
misconceptions: Speedometer measures average speed. Incorrect; odometer and clock give average.
Speedometer measures velocity. Incorrect; it lacks direction.
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Speedometer shows instantaneous speed, not average.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with average speed from odometer and trip time.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate between average and instantaneous speed
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Speedometer reads instantaneous speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: How does average speed differ from instantaneous speed?

options.A: Average speed includes direction; instantaneous does not
options.B: Average speed is over a time interval; instantaneous is at a moment
options.C: Instantaneous speed is always greater than average speed
options.D: Average speed is a vector; instantaneous is scalar

questions.hint: Average involves total time; instantaneous is at one instant.
questions.explanation: Average speed = total distance / total time over a finite interval. Instantaneous speed is the speed at a specific instant (limit as Δt → 0). Both are scalars. Option B is correct.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: What are the differences and similarities between average speed and instantaneous speed?

explanations_tiered.definition: Average speed is total distance over total time; instantaneous speed is speed at a moment.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Average speed summarizes a whole trip; instantaneous speed can vary during the trip.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Average speed is like your overall pace; instantaneous is your speed at any given second.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Average speed = S_total / t_total over an interval. Instantaneous speed = limit as Δt→0 of ΔS/Δt.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Instantaneous speed is the derivative of distance with respect to time: \(v = dS/dt\).
reasoning_steps: Note time scale difference: interval vs instant.
misconceptions: Average speed and instantaneous speed are always equal. Incorrect; they differ if speed varies.
Instantaneous speed can be negative. Incorrect; speed is magnitude, always ≥ 0.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Average = total/total; Instantaneous = at a moment.

flashcard.front: Difference between average and instantaneous speed?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Average is over a time interval; instantaneous is at a specific instant.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Average speed over interval; instantaneous at an instant.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does average speed differ from instantaneous?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Average over interval; instantaneous at a moment.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Average = total/total; Instant = at a moment.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a trip with varying speed to illustrate difference.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate between average and instantaneous speed
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Only B correctly states the fundamental difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: If a car is travelling at \(108\,\mathrm{km/h}\), what is its speed in \(\mathrm{m/s}\)?
questions.hint: Divide km/h by 3.6 to get m/s.
questions.explanation: \(108 / 3.6 = 30\,\mathrm{m/s}\). Option B would be multiplying by 1000/3600 incorrectly (108 × 1000/3600 = 30, not 300).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: If the car is travelling at 120 km/h what is the car's speed in m/s?

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: To convert km/h to m/s, divide by 3.6.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 108 divided by 3.6 is 30, so 108 km/h = 30 m/s.
misconceptions: Multiplying by 1000 and 3600 separately without dividing (108 × 1000/3600 = 30, but some multiply 108 × 1000 × 3600).
Using 3.6 incorrectly (multiplying instead of dividing).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: Is average speed the same as the magnitude of average velocity?

options.C: Only when motion is in a straight line without changing direction

questions.hint: Consider a round trip: average speed > 0, average velocity magnitude = 0.
questions.explanation: Average speed = total distance / total time; magnitude of average velocity = |displacement| / time. They are equal only when displacement magnitude equals distance, i.e., straight-line motion without direction change. Option C correctly states this condition.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Does the average speed the same as the magnitude of the average velocity? Explain.

explanations_tiered.definition: Average speed = total distance / total time; magnitude of average velocity = |displacement| / time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: They differ if the path is not a straight line in one direction (e.g., turning or returning).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you walk 10 m east then 10 m west back to start, your average speed is positive but average velocity is zero.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Distance ≥ |displacement|, so average speed ≥ |average velocity|. Equality holds only for straight-line motion without reversal.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Average speed is \(S_{tot}/t\); magnitude of average velocity is \(|\Delta \vec{S}|/t\). Since \(S_{tot} \ge |\Delta \vec{S}|\), equality only when motion is unidirectional straight line.
reasoning_steps: Compare total distance vs displacement magnitude.
misconceptions: They are always equal. Incorrect; counterexample: round trip.
They are never equal. Incorrect; they are equal for straight-line motion without direction change.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Avg speed = |avg velocity| only for straight-line one-way motion.

glossary_entry.definition: Equal only when motion is straight line without direction change.

flashcard.front: When is average speed equal to magnitude of average velocity?
flashcard.flashcard_back: When motion is straight line without changing direction.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer C. They are equal only for straight-line motion without direction reversal.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use round-trip example to illustrate inequality.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare average speed and average velocity magnitude

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: Tirunesh Dibaba ran 5000 m in 14 min 39.94 s. What was her average speed in m/s?
questions.hint: Convert time to seconds: 14 min = 840 s; total time = 879.94 s. Then speed = distance/time.
questions.explanation: Total time = 14 × 60 + 39.94 = 840 + 39.94 = 879.94 s. Speed = 5000/879.94 ≈ 5.68 m/s. Option B would be 5000/(14×60) ignoring the extra seconds.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Tirunesh Dibaba won... 5,000 metres in 14 min 39.94 sec. Calculate her average speed.

explanations_tiered.definition: Average speed = total distance / total time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Convert time to seconds correctly for unit consistency.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 14 minutes = 840 seconds, plus 39.94 gives 879.94 s. 5000 ÷ 879.94 ≈ 5.68 m/s.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Precision: using 879.94 s yields 5.682 m/s (to 4 sig figs).
misconceptions: Using 14.39 min directly (14 + 39.94/60 = 14.6657 min).
Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds (5000/14 = 357 m/s nonsense).

flashcard.front: 5000 m in 14 min 39.94 s: average speed (m/s)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 5000 m in 14 min 39.94 s: average speed?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize exact conversion of time to seconds.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compute average speed from real-world data
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. t = 879.94 s, v = 5.68 m/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: If the initial and final velocities of a car are the same, what is its acceleration?
questions.explanation: If v_f = v_i, then Δv = 0, so acceleration = 0 regardless of time. Option D is incorrect because we know Δv = 0, so a = 0.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: If the initial and final velocities of a car are the same, what will be its acceleration?

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration = rate of change of velocity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If velocity does not change, acceleration is zero.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you're going the same speed and direction, you're not accelerating — acceleration is zero.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(a = (v_f - v_i)/\Delta t\). If \(v_f = v_i\), numerator = 0, so \(a = 0\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Zero acceleration implies constant velocity, not necessarily zero velocity.
misconceptions: Acceleration could be positive or negative if v_f = v_i. No; Δv = 0 exactly.
Acceleration is zero only if time is large. No; Δv=0 gives a=0 regardless of Δt.

flashcard.front: If initial and final velocities are equal, acceleration = ?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that constant velocity implies zero acceleration.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Relate constant velocity to zero acceleration
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Since v_f = v_i, Δv = 0, so a = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of uniform motion?

options.A: A ball dropped from a height
options.B: A car moving at 20 m/s on a straight highway

questions.hint: Uniform motion = constant velocity (constant speed + straight line).
questions.explanation: Uniform motion requires constant speed and constant direction. Option B fits: constant speed on a straight road. Options A and D involve acceleration; option C changes direction.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: (No explicit content, but uniform motion defined as constant velocity)

explanations_tiered.definition: Uniform motion is motion with constant velocity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Uniform motion means no acceleration; speed and direction are constant.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A car going straight at the same speed without speeding up or turning is uniform motion.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Constant velocity implies \(a = 0\). A car at 20 m/s on a straight road has constant velocity.
misconceptions: Uniform motion includes constant speed in a circle. Incorrect; direction changes.
Falling objects have uniform motion. Incorrect; they accelerate due to gravity.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Uniform motion = constant speed + straight line.

flashcard.front: Which describes uniform motion: constant speed turning or constant speed straight?

concept_map_nodes.target: Car at constant speed on straight road

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Car at constant speed on straight road has constant velocity.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Car at constant speed on straight road.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Only B has constant speed and straight line.
